body_txt: When a couple gets married, the expectation in most cultures is for the wife to take her husband's surname. However, there can be cases where it's the husband who changes his surname. This is very uncommon in many countries around the world, but it's considered fairly normal in some, such as Japan (where spouses are legally required to have the same surname). It's more notable in western countries descended from French and English imperial culture, as it became the norm in the early modern period for women to take their husband's names in order for men to more firmly take control of any property she inherited (apart from some jewelry) and marking the woman's identity having been subsumed into the husband's as part of his property; having established the pattern for centuries, the deviation thus becomes much more significant. In cultures without that tradition, this trope may be absent or go unremarked. This trope applies when there was a significant reason for the husband changing his surname. Some possible cases are: A variation of this trope is when the idea is evoked as a show of love; much like a Fangirl or a Smitten Teenage Girl is dreaming about marrying her crush and taking his last name but applied to a male character. Unlike the female version though, it's much rarer to see a male character actually taking his wife's last name solely out of love, or being outright proud of it, unless under some of the aforementioned reasons. Compare and contrast The Maiden Name Debate where the wife has the option of keeping her maiden name while the husband keeps his own surname. Compare Nom de Mom where it's the married couple's child who takes the mother's maiden name. A subtrope of Shed the Family Name .
